quiz_questions.question_text: What is uniform motion?

options.text: Motion where the object covers equal distances in equal time intervals
Motion where the object speeds up and slows down repeatedly
Motion where the object only moves in a circular path always
Motion where the object changes direction at every moment

quiz_questions.explanation: Uniform motion means the object moves at a constant velocity, covering equal distances in equal time intervals. There is no acceleration in uniform motion since the speed and direction do not change.

quiz_questions.question_text: In non-uniform motion, what is changing?

options.text: The mass of the object changes
The velocity of the object changes
The color of the object changes
The size of the object changes

quiz_questions.explanation: In non-uniform motion, the velocity changes over time. The object may speed up, slow down, or change direction. This means the object is accelerating during non-uniform motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the acceleration of an object in uniform motion?

options.text: It is increasing over time
It is decreasing over time
It is zero at all times
It is always negative

quiz_questions.explanation: In uniform motion, the velocity is constant, which means it is not changing. Since acceleration is the rate of change of velocity, and velocity is not changing, the acceleration is zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a position-time graph look like for an object in uniform motion?

options.text: A curved line that bends upward
A horizontal flat line at zero
A zigzag pattern going up and down
A straight line with constant slope

quiz_questions.explanation: Uniform motion has a constant velocity, so the position changes at a steady rate. On a position-time graph, this appears as a straight line with a constant slope. The slope equals the velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a velocity-time graph look like for an object in uniform motion?

options.text: A horizontal straight line at a constant value
A line sloping upward from the origin
A curved line increasing over time
A line sloping downward to zero

quiz_questions.explanation: In uniform motion, velocity does not change. On a velocity-time graph, this is shown as a flat horizontal line because the velocity stays at the same value throughout the motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is an example of non-uniform motion?

options.text: A rock sitting still on a flat table
A train cruising at a steady 60 km/h
A car accelerating from a red traffic light
A conveyor belt moving at one set speed

quiz_questions.explanation: A car accelerating from a red light is changing its velocity (speeding up), so it is in non-uniform motion. The other options describe objects either at rest or moving at a constant speed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a curved line on a position-time graph indicate about the type of motion?

options.text: The object is in perfectly uniform motion
The object is in non-uniform motion
The object is completely at rest
The object has no mass at all

quiz_questions.explanation: A curved line on a position-time graph means the slope is changing, which means the velocity is changing. Changing velocity means the object is accelerating, which is the hallmark of non-uniform motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object travels 5 meters every second for 10 seconds. What type of motion is this?

options.text: Non-uniform motion with acceleration
Random motion without any pattern
Uniform motion at constant velocity
Circular motion around a central point

quiz_questions.explanation: Covering equal distances (5 m) in equal time intervals (1 s) is the definition of uniform motion. The velocity is constant at 5 m/s with zero acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: In non-uniform motion, is a net force acting on the object?

options.text: Yes, a net force is needed to change velocity
No, forces are only present when at rest
No, velocity changes on its own without force
Yes, but the force has nothing to do with it

quiz_questions.explanation: According to Newton's second law, a net force is required to cause acceleration. Since non-uniform motion involves changing velocity (acceleration), there must be a net force acting on the object.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes uniform motion in a straight line?

options.text: The object speeds up at a steady rate
The object moves with constant speed and direction
The object slows down at a steady rate
The object reverses direction periodically

quiz_questions.explanation: Uniform motion in a straight line means the object maintains both constant speed and constant direction. This means the velocity vector stays the same, with zero acceleration throughout the motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is uniform motion?

options.text: Motion in which velocity remains constant over time
Motion in which the object continually accelerates
Motion that occurs only in a circular path
Motion that requires no force to get started

quiz_questions.explanation: Uniform motion is motion in which the velocity (both speed and direction) remains constant. The object covers equal distances in equal time intervals and has zero acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is non-uniform motion?

options.text: Motion where velocity stays the same at all times
Motion where the object is always stationary
Motion where no forces are acting on the object
Motion where velocity changes over time

quiz_questions.explanation: Non-uniform motion is motion in which the velocity changes. The object may speed up, slow down, or change direction. It covers unequal distances in equal time intervals.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'constant velocity' mean?

options.text: The object moves with the same speed in the same direction
The object is gaining speed at a uniform steady rate
The object is losing speed at a uniform steady rate
The object is stationary and has zero velocity value

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant velocity means both the speed and the direction of an object remain unchanged over time. The object is neither speeding up, slowing down, nor turning.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'acceleration' mean in the context of non-uniform motion?

options.text: The object is not moving at all in any direction
The rate at which velocity changes over time
The total distance covered in one full second
The mass of the object times its current height

quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes over time. In non-uniform motion, the object has a non-zero acceleration because its velocity is not staying constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'uniform acceleration' mean?

options.text: The velocity stays the same at all times
The object is completely at rest throughout
The object reaches its maximum speed instantly
Velocity changes by equal amounts in equal times

quiz_questions.explanation: Uniform acceleration means the velocity changes at a constant rate. For example, if an object gains 2 m/s every second, it has uniform acceleration of 2 m/s squared.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'variable acceleration' mean?

options.text: The object moves at a constant velocity throughout
The rate of velocity change itself changes over time
The object has exactly zero acceleration at all times
The object only moves in one specific direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Variable acceleration means the acceleration is not constant; it changes over time. On a velocity-time graph, this appears as a curved line because the slope is different at different points.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'equal time intervals' in the definition of uniform motion?

options.text: Segments of time that are all the same duration
The total time it takes for the entire journey
The exact moment when the object starts moving
The distance between two points along the path

quiz_questions.explanation: Equal time intervals are segments of time that have the same duration, such as every 1 second or every 5 seconds. In uniform motion, the object covers the same distance during each of these equal time intervals.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'net force' mean in the context of uniform and non-uniform motion?

options.text: The mass of the object multiplied by its volume
The overall combined force acting on an object
The velocity of the object at a specific time
The distance traveled during one time interval

quiz_questions.explanation: Net force is the sum of all forces acting on an object. In uniform motion, the net force is zero (balanced forces). In non-uniform motion, there is a non-zero net force causing acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'deceleration' mean?

options.text: An increase in velocity over a time period
Motion at a constant speed in one direction
A decrease in the speed of an object over time
The total distance covered by a moving object

quiz_questions.explanation: Deceleration is the decrease in speed over time. It is a type of non-uniform motion where the object slows down. Deceleration is sometimes called negative acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'inertia' mean in relation to uniform motion?

options.text: The force that makes objects accelerate quickly
The speed at which light travels through space
The distance an object covers every single second
An object's tendency to resist changes in motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. An object in uniform motion tends to stay in uniform motion unless acted upon by a net external force. This is Newton's first law.

quiz_questions.question_text: A conveyor belt moves packages at a steady 2 m/s without stopping. What type of motion is this?

options.text: Non-uniform motion with changing velocity
Uniform motion with constant velocity
Accelerated motion with increasing speed
Circular motion around a fixed center

quiz_questions.explanation: The conveyor belt moves at a steady 2 m/s without changing speed or direction. This is uniform motion because the velocity is constant and the belt covers equal distances in equal time intervals.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car speeds up from 0 to 60 km/h in 10 seconds. Is this uniform or non-uniform motion?

options.text: Uniform motion because it is on a road
Uniform motion because 10 seconds is short
Non-uniform motion because velocity is changing
Neither uniform nor non-uniform motion

quiz_questions.explanation: The car's velocity is changing from 0 to 60 km/h, which means it is accelerating. Any motion with changing velocity is non-uniform motion, regardless of where it occurs.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball rolls down a hill and covers 2 m in the first second, 4 m in the second, and 6 m in the third. What type of motion is this?

options.text: Uniform motion at constant speed
The ball is not moving at all
Random motion with no real pattern
Non-uniform motion with acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: The ball covers different distances each second (2 m, 4 m, 6 m), meaning it is speeding up. Since the velocity is changing, this is non-uniform motion. The ball accelerates due to gravity pulling it downhill.

quiz_questions.question_text: An airplane flies at a constant altitude and a steady 800 km/h for 3 hours. On a velocity-time graph, what would this look like?

options.text: A line that slopes upward continuously
A curved line getting steeper over time
A flat horizontal line at 800 km/h
A line that slopes downward from 800

quiz_questions.explanation: The airplane maintains a steady 800 km/h, which is uniform motion. On a velocity-time graph, constant velocity appears as a flat horizontal line at the velocity value of 800 km/h.

quiz_questions.question_text: A skateboarder rolls on flat ground at 3 m/s, then goes downhill and speeds up to 8 m/s. Which part of the trip is non-uniform motion?

options.text: The downhill part where speed changed from 3 to 8 m/s
The flat ground part at the steady 3 m/s
Both parts are uniform motion throughout the trip
Neither part involves non-uniform motion at all

quiz_questions.explanation: The flat ground part at 3 m/s is uniform motion (constant velocity). The downhill part where speed changes from 3 to 8 m/s is non-uniform motion because the velocity is increasing.

quiz_questions.question_text: A toy train moves around a track at a steady speed of 1 m/s but the track is circular. Is this uniform or non-uniform motion?

options.text: Uniform because the speed stays constant always
Non-uniform because the direction keeps changing
Neither uniform nor non-uniform in this case
It depends on how large the circle track is

quiz_questions.explanation: Even though the speed is constant, the direction is continuously changing on a circular track. Velocity includes both speed and direction, so changing direction means changing velocity, making this non-uniform motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student records a car's position every second: 10 m, 20 m, 30 m, 40 m, 50 m. What type of motion is this?

options.text: Non-uniform because the positions are different
Random motion that cannot be classified at all
Decelerating motion that is slowing down each second
Uniform motion covering 10 m each second

quiz_questions.explanation: The car moves 10 m every second (10, 20, 30, 40, 50). Equal distances in equal time intervals means uniform motion at a constant velocity of 10 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bus slows down as it approaches a bus stop. On a velocity-time graph, what would this look like?

options.text: A horizontal line at a constant velocity value
A line sloping upward from zero continuously
A line sloping downward toward zero velocity
A vertical line jumping from high to low

quiz_questions.explanation: The bus is decelerating (slowing down), which is non-uniform motion. On a velocity-time graph, slowing down appears as a line sloping downward, moving toward zero velocity as the bus comes to a stop.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two cars start from rest. Car A reaches 30 m/s in 10 seconds. Car B reaches 30 m/s in 5 seconds. Both accelerate uniformly. Which has greater acceleration?

options.text: Car A because it took more time to reach 30 m/s
Car B because it reached 30 m/s in less time
Both have the same acceleration since same speed
Neither is accelerating since they both started still

quiz_questions.explanation: Car A: acceleration = 30/10 = 3 m/s squared. Car B: acceleration = 30/5 = 6 m/s squared. Car B has greater acceleration because it reached the same speed in less time, meaning its velocity changed faster.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cyclist rides at 5 m/s on a flat road, then 5 m/s up a hill (same speed). Is the motion uniform throughout if the road is straight?

options.text: Yes, because the speed stays at 5 m/s the whole time
No, because going uphill always means acceleration
No, because the cyclist must be getting tired
Yes, but only the flat portion counts as motion

quiz_questions.explanation: If the cyclist maintains the same speed (5 m/s) and the road is straight (same direction), then the velocity is constant throughout. This is uniform motion, even if the road goes uphill, as long as speed and direction do not change.
